sentences.text: In quarantine, they said was quarantine, but to me, was not quarantine, because they were taking us, some of us, they were taking us to work outside.
It cannot be quarantine and to take you out to work.
And what kind of work?
Get that son from here.
Put them over there.
And get that son over there.
Put them back then over there.
Bring him back again.
Or the post of the Germans, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] now, they were-- it could be fixed, and so on, something like that.
Or cut a tree.
Anyway, after that, that was the quarantine.
But I was hiding all the time when I-- I was seeing the Germans coming to take us to work because I didn't want to go to work for them.
And I was hiding all the time.
Dario too, his brother, older brother Dario's was not so lucky.
He was hiding and always was getting him to go to work.
It was coming in the car park in the evening was coming with a head like this of the beating your head from the Germans.
With me.
And Dario, sure.
Year was 1943 when we got there, '43, '44.
I don't remember exactly, yeah.
I think it was '44, yeah.

And the only problem we had in quarantine, Carol, was that we have in every-- in each barrack was a regular barrack from some other prisoners too, there were beds, one or two or three [? lays, ?] or beds, wooden beds, with some kind of-- I don't know how they call them.
They're some kind of a dry grass.
Straw, yeah was straw over there, maybe one blanket.
And it was so cold, so cold that every 30 minutes, we have to get up and go and leg for pain.
What do they call them, every 30 minutes.
And those bathrooms over there, which what bathroom, it was a-- was something with cement done over there.
And everybody going pee over there and so on.

So we couldn't-- we couldn't have not a little sleep at a time, because every 30 minutes, eight times and more than that, I was getting up, myself and many other people, from the cold because we didn't have no heat over there from the Poland cold, going in those bathrooms.
So how could we go a and work at that time.
That's why we were hiding, and I could do to go to work.
And going hiding was afraid that we're going to get killed if they find us.
That was the quarantine.
And after that, they took us to the crematoriums.
What kind of food did they give you while you were in quarantine?
Pfft!
Food, we're not talking about food.
Food, losing weight every day.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The food was kind of radishes, some big radishes like this-- they used to have in Poland and German.
I don't know-- with water because they put into some margarine, I guess.
But in the kitchen, the-- the cooks, they were taking away the-- the margarine.
They were [INAUDIBLE].
This was a kind of a soup, water soup with the radishes, and a little piece, maybe-- maybe two slices of bread, thick like this, with little piece of margarine.
That was for 24 hours.
Everybody hungry, and everybody dying from hungry.
We used to call the people they-- they were hungry like this.
They couldn't walk any more was-- they were called Muselmanns.
Call them because they were so-- so thin at the end, they were taking to us in the crematories, burning them.
How many people?
I know we went over there when-- I don't remember, [?
Carol, ?] how many people there were in quarantine, because we were not only by the Greeks only.
Some other people they were going out, and others coming in.
That was going out all the time.
More in towards out of the quarantine and some other people coming in.
So I don't know how many can-- could it be in the quarantine.
Was is it?
Too many, too many.
I didn't [INAUDIBLE].
Were so many barracks, maybe 1,000, 2,000.
In the north barrack?
Yeah.
In the barrack?
Oh, In the barrack was, who knows how many beds it was.
It was three layers of bed, four people in-- in every bed.
Maybe it was 200.
No, not all Greek.
When they took us over there, they most was Greeks, of course.
But were some-- were some even from some young people I saw over there, Russians.
No, no, because everybody over there, because some new people were coming in the quarantine, the other older ones, always trying to steal something from the new ones.
Like, for example, we woke up one morning.
I was with Dario.
And Dario's shoes was not there.
And Dario was with no shoes.
Can you imagine now, just walking with the snow over there with no shoes?
So and he saw a young Russian stealing his shoes.
And he couldn't do nothing because the Russian run away, and he was stronger than Dario.
Then Dario called me.
I was much stronger than Dario at the time.
And I went and I got the Russian.
I took the shoes, and I punched the Russian right in his face.
And we ran away because some of the Russian's people were coming to get us.
So that's the only way Dario took me to him, because he had fed me, Dario, because I was much stronger than him.
So this what happened in the quarantine.
Everybody was trying to steal something else from somebody else, a cloth or a-- a hat, or shoes, or whatever.
So about how long were you in quarantine?
How long would you say, weeks, a month?
It took us maybe two weeks, three weeks, something like that.
I don't remember exactly everything, [?
Carol. ?]
No.
They took us right away to the crematoriums.

So what we saw on the crematoriums and I was in crematorium three, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] what we saw over there for the first time, was over there four crematories-- I could make the design of it, because I got them in my mind-- was four crematories, four ovens in the crematorium, which every crematorium took about three people in it.
And it took about 15 to 20 minutes to burn up.
Three, the most was three.
Always, we used to put a woman in the center and two men by the sides, because with the flame, the woman is much fat than the men.
So the flames was going to the men's body, and they were burning all the-- all the way at the same time.
That was-- the gentlemen, the SS men, they had preventing everything.
They were thinking about everything.
It was everything was working like a watch, like a clock.
The oldest ones of Polands over there was much stronger than we were because we're big people and stronger that they were showing us how to do it.
As a matter of fact, I was working with Dario at the time.
Greek.
With whom?
Oh, to the Polish people?
With the hand, with hands, and making movements, something, because we couldn't understand them.
They couldn't understand us.
So they-- they-- they think they show they-- we saw the first time when we went in the crematorium was the four ovens with three people in it all the time, three dead bodies.
And from kind of a big elevator from downstairs of the bunker calling was a gas chamber.
Some of the people, prisoners, pulling the dead bodies on the elevator, one on top of the other-- who knows how many they could pull over there-- that elevator coming up.
There was the ovens.
And some other people or prisoners, like us, the most of the time were Jewish-- was Greeks, and I'll tell you why.

They took us to put them in the crematoriums with the Greeks, because they had about-- in this crematorium, there was about 100 people working in each crematorium when before, why they took about 300, 400 people from Greece.
And I'll tell you why.
So I'll finish first.
What we saw in the beginning, so was a space in front of the ovens, a cemented space, too long for about maybe-- they used to put three-- three dead bodies in each line.
Like that was about 10 lines, about 30 people, 30 dead bodies in each line.
So we didn't know what to say with Dario.

We were going to take the-- we was going to-- to take the dead bodies, to put them down there from the elevator, to put them down there, and holding their head, not-- not the heads of the dead bodies to get heat from the cement.
Instead, we could-- we could saw-- the Poland people getting in the bodies, and just to threw it, just like it were like a piece of junk.
Thrown them in.
They did-- they didn't care.
But we were new over there.
We didn't know.
We were afraid that we could believe what was going-- what were happening.

And when the kapos came in with a stick on it and to show us to take them like they were taking the other guys were taken and threw it like a piece of-- of junk on the floor, we started doing the same thing, not because-- not to get beaten.
And where there were all this line about 30 people was right on the floor over there, maybe it was 36 people.
It really doesn't matter.
I don't remember exactly how many.
I didn't count it.
A couple a Jewish prisoners again with a gun, they were from France, or maybe those ones, they were not from France.
Anyway, they had in their hands a kind of pliers.
And was taking out all the teeth of the dead bodies.
At that time, everybody used to have golden teeth.
And we stayed-- they were taking-- there was take all day the teeth from the dead bodies.
And after that, that we said, Dario and me, taking one by one of them, put them in the what they call them?
This-- they got them for sick people in their emergencies.
Stretcher.
Stretcher.
Take and put them on the stretcher, sometime in two bodies together, depend on the size the bodies, opened the door of the oven.
And somebody's pushing the stretcher in there.
And somebody else holding with a kind of a piece of wire on, holding the bodies to take off the stretcher.
And they start burning.
Like I said before, they were putting one woman on the center, just to get the flames to the dead bodies of the men.
That was almost happening every week, 3,000 of Jewish people coming from different places.
But when we-- when we was not 3,000-- the transport were not coming all the time-- they used to bring about 200, 300 from inside of the-- of the camp.
And that was-- kill-- they used to kill them not with the gas in the gas chamber, because the gas chamber was for 3,000, just to get it inside, like a piece of sardines.
And that's the way-- the way those 6,000 people they were killed with asphyxia call them?
Somebody from the [INAUDIBLE] was thrown in the-- this kind of a gas.
I don't know why they call them.
Zyklon B?
Anyway, so they would kill them [INAUDIBLE] of the bunker downstairs, those 200 or 300 people with a-- with a German, with a shotgun, one by one.
One of us used to hold the prisoner from the ear and tell him-- and tell him, because they didn't know what was going on, and now they were going to kill.
Holding the ear like this, and the-- hiding [INAUDIBLE] of the-- of the German, just shoot him in the back of the head.
And, of course, when they shoot somebody in the back of the head, the knees bended, and the body goes in the back, not in the front.
That's why the one was holding the guy by the ear.
When the German was shotting him, he used to push him like this, so the body was going at the front.
And right away, somebody else, it was with a hose of water, cleaning the blood.
So somebody was coming again, the same story, the same story, same story.
It happened me to be one day over there, and I didn't know that I had to push the man not to fall in the back.
And usually, the men who was down, the German, they says-- they told us, or he told us-- he was very friendly with us.
That gunner says, was very friendly, smoking together, giving us a cigarette, or give him a cigarette, very friendly.
It was something else, because all the other ones, they was hitting us with a stick, with a bat, stick, what they call it?
I don't know.
What they call them, this?
Like a baton, like a truncheon?
Somebody or limps or cannot--
Cane.
So all the-- those Germans, they were hitting us with a cane on the head.
We were getting away so quickly because our head was becoming so enlarged from the-- from the cane.
So this guy was the only one who was so friendly with us, and the only one when they would come in 200 or 300s of those people to get killed, this guy was the first one to go and kill them.
Was something [? unbelievable. ?] So friendly with us.
We were-- we were Jewish.
And he had the pleasure to go and shot 200 or 300 just-- just for fun.
And happened me, I was-- I didn't know, like I said before, to push him this way.
And I was there, And I was-- and I hold his-- his ear of the guy.
And I told him, look.
[INAUDIBLE] after the guy to look in front of him.
Bang, they shot.
And I got scared, and I left him.
I didn't push him, any he go.
He went back.
And at that time was not this Holland guy from the SS, was somebody else.
Soon as the dead body fell in the back, from the blood spill of his head, he dirty his boots with blood.
And right away, got the shot gun to shoot me because the blood fell on his boot.
And thank God that my brother was there.
And my brother he has some acquaintance with this guy because always my brother was there holding the ear.
Nobody else could do that, that thing.
And thank God he said, oh, my brother, my brother, my brother.
And this is the only way he didn't shot me.
And right away, the other guy with the hose, he went.
We cleaned the-- the German boot, and so on.
And that was the first and the last time I was in that place holding the ear from somebody else.
When that thing was coming, I was hiding, getting away much as I could.
And I never went back again to see the-- to see those thing.
From Holland, SS from Holland.
No.
They never told us names.
Why they should ask our names?
We didn't have no names either.
We had the numbers.
Put them in the elevator.
They come upstairs with the oven.
Of course, I was every day over there.
It's nothing to describe.
It was an empty room.
You can imagine for 3,000 people to go in there.
I can tell you that, that soon as the transfer came-- was coming, like I told you, my mother was in lines [INAUDIBLE] with the kids and the pregnant women and so on, they were taken to us.
What they were taken to us, they used to put them in some other rooms before.
And the German was telling them to take off their clothes.
Another trick over there, telling them to tie up the shoes together and to put all their clothes together because they're going to come out from the shower to find the shoes and the clothes again.
That was a trick from the Germans.
So when all those women they were naked, they were going from a corridor right in the gas chamber.
And the women, of course, they were ashamed to be naked ladies.
And they were going one to another so close.
And the kids near to the mothers, you, know.
And everybody screaming and so on and so on.
And they were going right in the gas chamber.
In the meantime, in the back of it from another room, they were come naked the men.
And when the women was seeing the men naked, they would start again screaming.
It's a shame.
It's a shame.
It's a shame.
So when there was the men hearing the women screaming, the German with a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
With the canes, with a cane, hitting the mens on the head.
They would [INAUDIBLE] where they could hit.
[INAUDIBLE] And the men, they were going to get away.
And they were going with the women together.
When everybody was in, close the door.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In the beginning, I was there.
After they close the door, I could hear the 3,000 people, voices crying and screaming.
And you could hear [SPEAKING HEBREW].
They were calling God.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Nothing happened.
Never nothing happened.
And these voices still in my ears.
But any other transport they were coming, I was hiding again.
I was getting too far away not to hear those voices calling God.
I never went back again.
But if a soldier or a German SS was find me hiding, they could shoot.
But I couldn't stand on those voices.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] After that, elevator again, up, pull them into this corridor, taking off the gold teeth, and in the ovens.
That was four crematoriums.
Everywhere was happening that.
But we not were-- were working 24 hours all the time.
I was almost regular hours, eight hours, 10 hours.
But we had food over there.
Of the people coming in, you could find cookies.
We could find gold.
We could find in the-- toys, stuffed toys, we could find inside diamonds, and so on.
And this way, we could make something with this.
You're given to our kapo.
The kapo give them to the German.
The German going into the Auschwitz town and bring us a piece of salami or a piece of bread, like happened with the two golden coins we swallow.
At the end, we get it that way for a piece of bread and a piece of salami.
That's what happened with the two golds we swallowed two times.
We, prisoners.
Working in the crematories.
How?
Well, it was not so easy to take them out either because they were all on the bended knees and heads, because they were like sardines inside.
It was very difficult to pick them up out. with a-- with a cane getting from the head and push them out or with belts, tied them up in one hand, and push them out again.
That's the only way we could take the dead bodies from the gas chamber.
And was funny and the cars jumping when the car was coming with the gas, they had a-- a Red Cross.
Yeah-- yeah it was gone.
Ventilation, ventilation, because the guys want to-- it was to take them out at three, four minutes.
Ventilation, was nothing, and we were not going right away.
And I remember one day, three Germans SS girls came in with some officers, SS officers, no.
And the transport just came.
And everybody was inside dying with the gas.
And those three girls from their SS went from the little window, was a little windows on the doors, like they had on the ships, those kind of windows, looking out the window, out.
They were looking out the-- those people, they were dying, and they were laughing.
They were laughing something unbelievable.
How could women, girls, could see such a thing and laughing?
They were so much brainwashed from that-- I don't want to say the word-- that Hitler, that animal.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Now, what were they doing with like this?
In the crematoria-- I don't remember if it were two or three, it doesn't matter which one it was it-- was three French guys, Jewish.
They were specialists in gold.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They had all the things they needed over there, all the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what they call them over there?
Like I told you, my English sometime-- all the tools to melt those teeth.
And they knew what kind of gold it is, [INAUDIBLE].

And almost-- I don't remember exactly-- every two weeks, or every month, or every week-- I don't remember exactly-- somebody of the SS man with a motorcycle was coming to the crematoriums and getting 10 kilos of gold.
All the time when he was coming, every month, I guess, 10 kilos of gold.
How they were doing it, the French guys, they was melting the gold and making four-- four pieces of each piece of 250 grams each, which four pieces of those make one kilo.
They were making 40 pieces of this gold, which was 10 kilos.
That was every week or every month come in and get it, the Germans from there, from the Jewish people.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now, we had good food because we could find from the transports a lot of food, a lot of diamonds, a lot of gold, because everybody was hiding something.
Like my mother gave me the five gold pieces.
Even in the cookies they were hiding even in the cookies some gold.
We were finding because we knew.
And the shoes, we could find it.
We knew on the clothes.
And this way, we had something good to eat, salami, bread, which was our life.
Yeah, go ahead.
